food_subgroup: Pan-fried open-face egg omelette with spiced mutton mince on a French baguette half, served with chilli sauce and mayonnaise

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Roti John as a prepared Singapore Malay hawker snack. No exact or close FoodOn term identified for this composite fried egg-and-meat baguette sandwich.
ontology.foodex2_description: Filled bread with egg and meat, fried (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A057M used as closest parent for a fried filled bread product with egg and meat. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Roti John specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Roti John as a prepared hawker food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Roti John is approximately 200g, comprising half a French baguette pressed onto a spiced minced mutton and beaten egg omelette, griddle-fried in oil, and served with chilli sauce and mayonnaise.

nutrient_source.title: Roti John — HPB Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database and USDA SR Legacy Component Calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore and USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roti John and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data for French baguette, minced mutton, egg, and condiments. Values represent a composite calculation for the full dish per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white bread and baguette analogous estimate; Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roti John. GI estimated at 65 using white French baguette as the primary carbohydrate base (GI ~75 per University of Sydney GI Database for white baguette/French bread). GI moderated downward to approximately 65 due to the protein content from egg and minced mutton and the fat from frying oil and mayonnaise, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated per 200g serving: (65 × 42g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.3; per 100g: (65 × 21g) / 100 = 13.65, rounded to 13.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 850mg per 200g serving from multiple combined sources including baguette salt, mutton seasoning, chilli sauce, and mayonnaise
hypertension.guidance: One 200g serving of Roti John contributes approximately 850mg sodium (~35–43% of the WHO 2000mg daily limit), making it a high-sodium snack that hypertensive patients should consume infrequently and pair with low-sodium meals for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) from refined white baguette base; glycemic load approximately 27 per 200g serving, equivalent to approximately 1.3 cups of cooked white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white baguette base gives Roti John a medium GI of approximately 65; the egg and mutton protein and frying fat partially moderate the glycaemic response, but the glycemic load of ~27 per serving is significant for T2DM patients managing carbohydrate intake and should be counted as a high-carbohydrate snack.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (5g per 200g serving), high sodium (~850mg per serving), and cholesterol (~160mg per serving) from egg and mutton
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roti John contains moderate saturated fat (~5g per serving) and high sodium (~850mg per serving) from the fried egg-mutton filling and condiments; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and request reduced condiments to lower sodium and fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Haem iron from mutton (~3mg per 200g serving), vitamin B12 (~1mcg per serving), and culinary-dose curcumin from curry spices
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roti John provides haem iron (~3mg per 200g serving, ~17% male RDA) and vitamin B12 (~1mcg) from mutton, and culinary-dose curcumin from curry powder; saturated fat is moderate at ~5g per serving and no strongly pro-inflammatory ingredients are present at typical serving size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roti John is a popular Singapore Malay hawker snack and street food, widely enjoyed across Singapore, Malaysia, and Brunei. The dish consists of a halved French baguette-style roll pressed face-down onto a hot flat griddle over a mixture of beaten eggs, spiced minced mutton (or beef), diced onions, and curry spices. The egg mixture cooks and adheres to the bread surface, forming a unified omelette-bread sandwich. Once the egg is set and the bread is lightly toasted and golden, the sandwich is flipped, then dressed with chilli sauce and mayonnaise, and optionally garnished with cucumber and lettuce before being sliced into bite-sized portions. The texture combines a crispy-edged, oil-toasted bread exterior with a soft, savoury egg-and-mutton omelette interior. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and rich, with the chilli sauce adding heat and the mayonnaise adding creaminess. Nutritionally, a 200g serving provides approximately 450 kcal, 20g protein, 20g fat, and 42g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at approximately 850mg per serving from multiple sources: baguette salt, mutton seasoning, chilli sauce, and mayonnaise. GI is estimated at 65 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving. The dish is universally halal (mutton, no pork). Clinically, it is a high-sodium snack relevant to hypertension management and a moderate-GI, high-GL carbohydrate load relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. Origin is debated but widely attributed to Singapore Malay hawker culture in the 1970s–1980s. The name is a colloquial reference to a generic Caucasian ('John') who reportedly requested an egg-and-meat omelette in a bun. Source: HPB Singapore and USDA SR Legacy.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients. The dish is a well-documented Singapore hawker item with consistent composition across multiple approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white French baguette as the primary carbohydrate base, with documented protein-fat moderation rationale.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite hawker dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite fried dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA SR Legacy for this preparation). Glycemic load per serving recalculated at 200g serving size: (65 × 42g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.3. The input record listed two default portions (350g and 250g); the canonical serving size of 200g (half baguette with filling, per HPB Singapore) is used as the primary serving for GL calculation. The 350g portion (full baguette with condiments) is noted as an alternative larger serving. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.1mg/100g), and vitamin_k (3.0mcg/100g) values are sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9; these are consistent with USDA SR Legacy component estimates for similar fried egg-and-meat bread products. Health suitability note: suitable flags from the input record are preserved but clinical guidance has been revised to be more specific and actionable per Veritas v2 requirements. The 'suitable: true' flag for hypertension in the input is noted as potentially misleading given the high sodium content; the health_context entry for hypertension is marked 'high' concern level in this output.
